1: "Observe the month of Abib and celebrate the Passover to the LORD your God, for in the month of Abib the LORD your God brought you out of Egypt by night" — Abib (H24, later called Nisan) is the first month of the Hebrew religious calendar, corresponding to March-April. "Abib" means "ear of grain" — the new-grain month, when barley begins to ripen. The name encodes the agricultural cycle into the calendar: Israel's liberation-event falls in the agricultural year's first month, tying covenant history to creation's rhythms. The Passover's centralization at the chosen sanctuary (v2) reflects the Deuteronomic reform program's consolidation of worship — historically, Passover was a household observance (Exodus 12:3-7); the centralization is a significant institutional development completed under Josiah (2 Kings 23:21-23).
2: "You shall sacrifice the Passover offering to the LORD your God — from the flock or the herd — at the place the LORD will choose to make his name dwell" — the inclusion of cattle (baqar) in addition to sheep and goats expands the Passover offering beyond Exodus 12's lamb-only provision. This may reflect the Passover's growth into a larger communal celebration at the central sanctuary where a single household's lamb would be inadequate for pilgrimage crowds. The "place the LORD will choose" (maaqom asher yivchar YHWH) is Deuteronomy's persistent formula for Jerusalem (the specific name is never given in Deuteronomy — the later Deuteronomistic History identifies it retroactively).
3: "You shall eat no leavened bread with it. For seven days you shall eat unleavened bread — the bread of affliction (lechem oni) — because you left the land of Egypt in haste" — the Feast of Unleavened Bread immediately follows and overlaps with Passover in Deuteronomy's presentation. The "bread of affliction" (lechem oni) names the unleavened bread's memorial function: it tastes of the haste of departure and the poverty of slavery. Archaeology has found traces of ancient flatbreads in Levantine contexts; unleavened bread was both the emergency food of hasty travel and the staple of pre-leavening culture. Leaven (chametz) in the NT becomes Paul's metaphor for the old life to be purged (1 Corinthians 5:6-8: "clean out the old leaven... for Christ our Passover has been sacrificed").
4: "No leaven shall be seen anywhere in your territory for seven days; and none of the flesh you sacrifice on the evening of the first day shall remain until morning" — the total leaven-removal from territory: the unleavened-bread feast is not merely a dietary restriction but a spatial cleansing of the entire land. The complete removal of leaven from all visible territory is a communal purification act. The injunction against leaving flesh from the first day overnight echoes the original Passover instructions (Exodus 12:10: nothing is to remain until morning) — the meal's completeness-in-one-night signals the definitive quality of the Passover event.
5: "You may not sacrifice the Passover within any of your towns that the LORD your God is giving you" — the centralization restriction: the Passover is removed from household and local practice and concentrated at the central sanctuary. This is the most significant institutional change from Exodus 12 to Deuteronomy 16: what was a household night-meal in Exodus becomes a pilgrimage festival in Deuteronomy. The Second Temple Passover (as described in the Mishnah tractate Pesachim) reflects this centralized structure — pilgrims came to Jerusalem, purchased animals, had them slaughtered in the temple precincts, roasted them in Jerusalem courtyards.
6: "Rather, at the place the LORD your God will choose to make his name dwell, there you shall sacrifice the Passover — in the evening at sunset, at the time you came out of Egypt" — the sunset-timing specified (baerev kevo hashemesh): the Passover begins at evening, consistent with the Hebrew day-structure (evening before morning) and the original Exodus night-event. The time-specification preserves the memorial function: the sacrifice begins as the sun sets, recalling the Exodus departure's darkness, the angel of death's midnight passage, and the dawn departure from Egypt.
7: "You shall cook and eat it at the place the LORD your God will choose, and in the morning you shall return to your tents" — the morning-return permission: unlike the seven-day Feast of Unleavened Bread (v8), the Passover meal itself is a one-night event after which pilgrims may return home. "Return to your tents" (ufanita lahaleicha) uses the archaic tent-language for home (a vestige of the wilderness period when Israel lived in tents), becoming a standard idiom for dismissal (cf. 1 Kings 12:16).
8: "For six days you shall eat unleavened bread, and on the seventh day there shall be a solemn assembly for the LORD your God — you shall do no work" — the seven-day Passover-Unleavened Bread complex concludes with a Sabbath-like assembly (atzeret). The structure mirrors the creation-week pattern: six days of eating/normal activity, the seventh a day of holy assembly. The atzeret (closing assembly) is a day of rest and worship — this pattern recurs at Shavuot (Leviticus 23:21) and Sukkot (Leviticus 23:36; Numbers 29:35). The agricultural festivals are embedded in Sabbath theology: the seventh-day rest extends into the seventh-day feast.
9: "Count off seven weeks. Start counting the seven weeks from when the sickle is first put to the standing grain" — Shavuot (Feast of Weeks) is counted from the beginning of the barley harvest. The fifty-day count (Pentecost = fifty, from Greek pentēkostē, fiftieth) begins at the cutting of the first grain and ends fifty days later at the wheat harvest. The agricultural grounding: Israel counts the weeks between barley harvest and wheat harvest and celebrates at each end. By the Second Temple period, Shavuot had been reinterpreted as the anniversary of the Sinai covenant — the giving of Torah fifty days after the Passover Exodus. Acts 2 takes place on Shavuot: the Spirit is given on the feast of Torah-giving, replacing the external law with the internal Spirit promised by Jeremiah and Ezekiel.
10: "Then you shall celebrate the Feast of Weeks to the LORD your God with a freewill offering from your hand — give in proportion to how the LORD your God has blessed you" — the Shavuot offering is a minat nedavat yadecha (freewill offering of your hand), not a fixed amount but proportional to blessing received. This proportionality principle appears at all three pilgrimage feasts (v17): the covenant's financial obligations scale with YHWH's provision, not as a flat tax. The freewill element acknowledges that the giver's heart-posture matters, not merely the transaction — generosity is the goal, not compliance.
11: "You shall rejoice before the LORD your God — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servants, the Levite within your towns, the foreigner, the fatherless, and the widow among you — at the place the LORD your God will choose to make his name dwell" — the seven-category inclusive celebration: the extended household (children, servants), the dependent class (Levites), the socially marginal (foreign resident, orphan, widow) are all explicitly commanded to rejoice before YHWH at the sanctuary. No member of the covenant community is excluded from the covenant's celebration. This radical inclusivity in Israelite worship contrasts with the stratified access of Egyptian temple religion (ordinary people had no access to the inner sanctuary; only priests approached the deity).
12: "Remember that you were a slave in Egypt, and be careful to observe these statutes" — the Exodus-memory grounds the Feast of Weeks as it grounded the Sabbath (Deuteronomy 5:15) and the slave-release law (Deuteronomy 15:15). The agricultural festivals are not merely harvest celebrations but covenant memorials: Israel's abundance in the land is YHWH's gift, understood in light of the Exodus from land-where-they-had-nothing. Gratitude for harvest is gratitude for redemption; the two cannot be separated in Deuteronomy's theological framework.
13: "You shall celebrate the Feast of Booths (sukkot) for seven days, when you have gathered in the produce from your threshing floor and your winepress" — Sukkot is the final harvest festival of the year, the seven-day celebration at the end of the agricultural year (late September-October) when all the grain has been threshed and the wine pressed. The booths (sukkot, temporary shelters) recalled the wilderness wandering when Israel lived in temporary shelters — the most abundant agricultural moment of the year is commemorated by recalling the most vulnerable, landless period of the nation's history. The contrast is theologically deliberate: from wandering in the desert to harvesting the land's abundance.
14: "You shall rejoice in your feast — you, your son and daughter, your male and female servants, the Levite, the sojourner, the fatherless, and the widow within your towns" — the same seven-category inclusivity as Shavuot (v11): every member of the community from the household head to the most marginal is included in the covenant joy. The repetition of this formula across the three feasts establishes it as a liturgical constant: each festival enacts the same covenantal inclusivity. The feast is not a family-only celebration but a community event that deliberately includes those without family or inheritance.
15: "For seven days you shall keep the feast to the LORD your God at the place he will choose, for the LORD your God will bless you in all your produce and in all the work of your hands, so that you will be altogether joyful" — the Sukkot blessing-promise: the harvest abundance is YHWH's blessing, and the joy commanded (vehayita ach sameach, "and you shall be altogether joyful") is the covenant community's appropriate response to it. John 7 places Jesus at this feast — the context for his water-of-life announcement (Deuteronomy 7:37-39). The Sukkot water-drawing ceremony (simchat beit hashoeva) at the Second Temple — celebrated with great joy — is the background for Jesus's invitation: "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink." YHWH's promised blessing becomes Jesus's personal invitation.
16: "Three times a year all your males shall appear before the LORD your God at the place he will choose: at the Feast of Unleavened Bread, at the Feast of Weeks, and at the Feast of Booths" — the three pilgrimage feasts (shalosh regalim): Unleavened Bread (spring, Passover-connected), Weeks (early summer, firstfruits), Booths (autumn, final harvest). The three annual pilgrimages structure the agricultural year around covenant ceremony. Jesus's family pilgrimage to Jerusalem for Passover (Luke 2:41: "his parents went to Jerusalem every year at the Feast of the Passover") is the practice of this command. Jesus's own major discourses in John (Deuteronomy 5 around a feast, Deuteronomy 6 near Passover, Deuteronomy 7-8 at Tabernacles, Deuteronomy 10 at Dedication) follow the pilgrim-feast calendar.
17: "Every man shall give as he is able, in proportion to the blessing the LORD your God has given him" — the proportionality formula closes the three-feast section: each man brings what he can according to YHWH's blessing on him. The principle of proportional giving based on received blessing is consistent throughout the law (10%; the tithe is proportional by definition) and resurfaces in Paul's collection principle (1 Corinthians 16:2: "each of you is to put something aside ... as he may prosper"; 2 Corinthians 9:7: "each one must give as he has decided in his heart, not reluctantly or under compulsion, for God loves a cheerful giver").
18: "You shall appoint judges and officials throughout all your towns that the LORD your God is giving you, according to your tribes, and they shall judge the people with righteous judgment" — the judicial system: local judges and officers in every city, organized by tribal territory. The Deuteronomic judicial reform is substantial: centralize worship at one sanctuary but decentralize justice administration into every community. The complementary institutions prevent both religious and judicial abuse — no single court monopolizes both the divine and human administration of justice. The Levitical priests and the central judge (Deuteronomy 17:8-12) function as the supreme court; the local judges handle ordinary cases.
19: "Do not pervert justice; do not show favoritism; do not accept a bribe — for a bribe blinds the eyes of the wise and twists the words of the righteous" — the three judicial prohibitions: (1) lo tateh mishpat (pervert justice), (2) lo takir panim (show favoritism, literally "do not recognize faces" — do not let social status affect judgment), (3) lo tiqach shochad (accept a bribe). The bribe-wisdom saying is a common ANE proverb: a bribe corrupts even good judgment (iveir einei chachamim, blinds the eyes of the wise). The Egyptian wisdom tradition Instruction of Ptahhotep and the Mesopotamian wisdom texts both warn against judicial bribery; the universality of the corruption problem makes the Deuteronomic prohibition culturally resonant.
20: "Justice, and justice alone, you shall pursue (tsedek tsedek tirdof) — so that you may live and possess the land the LORD your God is giving you" — the famous doubled tsedek: the repetition is the Torah's most emphatic form of intensification. "Justice, justice" does not mean two kinds of justice but the absolute, uncompromising pursuit of one justice. The verb radaf (pursue, chase) is hunting vocabulary — pursue justice as one hunts prey, with single-minded urgency. The land-possession condition attached to justice: the land's retention depends on the justice maintained within it. The prophets make this explicit: Amos, Isaiah, Micah, and Jeremiah all connect the land's loss (the Assyrian and Babylonian exiles) to the judicial corruption and social injustice that Deuteronomy 16:18-20 commanded against.
21: "You shall not set up any wooden Asherah pole beside the altar of the LORD your God that you build" — the Asherah prohibition in the context of the pilgrimage-feast commands: the same location (the chosen sanctuary) where Israel celebrates the three feasts must be free of the Canaanite goddess-pole. The Asherah pole (asherah, H842) represented the goddess Asherah, consort of El and/or Baal in Canaanite religion, associated with fertility and blessing. Archaeological finds confirm widespread Asherah veneration in Israel's history — the Kuntillet Ajrud inscriptions (ca. 800 BCE) mention "YHWH of Samaria and his Asherah," indicating some Israelites associated YHWH with an Asherah consort. Deuteronomy's flat prohibition aims to prevent exactly this syncretism at the central sanctuary.
22: "And do not erect a sacred stone pillar (matsevah), for the LORD your God hates these" — the standing-stone prohibition: matsevah (H4676) were upright stones used in Canaanite cult as symbols of divine presence. Interestingly, the patriarchs used standing stones to commemorate theophanic encounters (Genesis 28:18; Deuteronomy 31:45; Deuteronomy 35:14) and these are presented positively in Genesis. Deuteronomy 16:22's prohibition reflects the later covenant concern that standing stones are too easily identified with Canaanite cult practice — whatever their original Israelite usage, they carry the cultural freight of pagan worship. The archaeological record shows standing stones at Israelite cult sites (Hazor, Megiddo, Dan) and their prohibition became increasingly strict in the later prophetic tradition.
